gazette: General Directorate of Trade Research and Risk Assessment
title: The Apostille Convention comes into force in Canada
bodyHtml:
The statement released by the Canadian Ministry of Foreign Affairs indicates that Canada officially became a party to the "Apostille Convention," which eliminates the requirement for the authentication of foreign public documents, on May 16, 2023. It came into effect on January 11, 2024.

According to this, Canadian citizens and businesses can now use public documents issued in Canada, such as export transactions, company records, birth and marriage certificates, education-related documents, in more than 120 countries that are parties to the mentioned convention without the need for further authentication, including our country.

The "Apostille Convention" was signed by Turkey on May 8, 1962, and entered into force on September 29, 1985 (https://www.hcch.net/en/instruments/conventions/status-table/?cid=41). With the entry into force of the convention in Canada on January 11, 2024, Turkish citizens and businesses can similarly use public documents issued in Turkey in Canada without the need for additional authentication.

For more information on Canada's accession to the Apostille Convention and its entry into force, you can visit https://www.international.gc.ca/gac-amc/about-a_propos/services/authentication-authentification/apostille-convention.aspx?lang=eng.
